content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 17, 1992


Mr. Ruel J. Czach
148 Birch Avenue
Cayucos, CA 93430

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-503

Dear Mr. Czach:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter only addresses the future
conduct of the Cayucos Citizens Advisory Council. The Commission
will not comment on any past conduct. (See Regulation
18329(c)(4)(A).)

QUESTION

Are members of the Cayucos Citizens Advisory Council "public
officials" subject to the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION

Because the Cayucos Citizens Advisory Council makes
substantive recommendations to the San Luis Obispo County Board of
Supervisors that are regularly approved without significant
modification, the Council is a decision-making body and its
members are public officials as defined by the Act. Consequently,
the members are subject to the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act.

FACTS

Your letter provided us with the Bylaws of the Cayucos
Citizens Advisory Council and information from the County of San
Luis Obispo. In addition, on March 19, 1992, you conveyed by

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Sections
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-503
Page 2


telephone other information to our Legal Intern, Chris Micheli, in
response to follow-up questions.

Although it was started in the late 1920s, the Council was
reactivated in the early 1970s. You have served on the Council
for four years and currently hold the position of Vice President
of the Council. Members are elected in a community-wide election
in March of each year to serve a one-year term. There are seven
precincts in your town and two members and an alternate are
elected to each precinct.

In addition, there are representatives of several service
organizations picked by their group to serve on the Council.
There have been discussions about eliminating these appointed
positions, as well as about consolidating the March election with
other local or national elections.

The Council meets once a month at the community hall for a
public meeting to discuss issues or concerns which are brought to
your attention, either through the public or by members of the
Council. The Council considers and votes on actions to take to
address local concerns, such as parking and property development.

The Council also prepares a Land Use Element and General Plan
update report including a policy statement to guide the planning
department in preparing and amending your local general plan. In
recent years, the Council has also examined and made
recommendations to approve or disapprove local development
projects and land use ordinance changes, among other things.

The Bylaws of the Council set forth the following purposes:

Section 1: To develop a unified, cooperative
effort among all individuals, organizations and
public jurisdictions interested in furthering sound
planning and development in the Cayucos area.

Section 2: To establish an organization truly
representative of the people of Cayucos in matters
of civic interest, and to represent the community
before all bodies, public and private, where the
subject is appropriate to the objectives of the
Cayucos Citizens Advisory Council.

Section 3: To establish a forum for the public
review of matters historically identified with
sound development including, but not limited to,
land use, public services, circulation, zoning,
improvements and all aspects of orderly
public improvements and all aspects of orderly
community growth.

Section 4: To initiate, sponsor, review and make
recommendations, particularly concerning all
matters of significance affecting the community.

Our File No. A-91-503
Page 3


After the Council makes recommendations to the Board of
Supervisors, the Board holds a public hearing and then votes on
the matter. In essence, it is free to accept or disregard the
Council's recommendation. However, in the four years you have
served on the Council, you cannot recall a single instance when
the Board has voted against the Council's recommendation.

Sometimes members of the Council have voted for these types
of projects, even though they have had a direct financial interest
in the project in the neighborhood. You expressed concern that
this situation may lead to people running for office on the
Council only to get their particular project approved.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California in 1974. The purpose of the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act was to ensure that public
officials, whether elected or appointed, would perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own
economic interest or the economic interests of persons who have
supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

The General Rule

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official, or on a member of the official's immediate family, or on
any of the official's economic interests.

Public Official

A public official includes every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a local or state
government agency. (Section 82048.) A "public official" includes
members of boards and commissions and is defined in Regulation
18700(a)(1) as follows:

(a) Public official at any level of state or
local government means every natural person who is
a member, officer, employee or consultant of a
state or local government agency.

(1) Member shall include, but not be
limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
boards or commissions with decision-making
authority. A board or commission possesses
decision-making authority whenever:

Our File No. A-91-503
Page 4


                    (A) It may make a final
            governmental decision;

                    (B) It may compel a governmental
            decision; or it may prevent a
            governmental decision either by reason of
            an exclusive power to initiate the
            decision or by reason of a veto which may
            not be overriden; or

                    (C) It makes substantive
            recommendations which are, and over an
            extended period of time have been,
            regularly approved without significant
            amendment or modification by another
            public official or governmental agency.

                    [Emphasis added.]

    Accordingly, if the Council meets any of the tests of
Regulation 18700(a)(1)(A), (B) or (C), it possesses decision-
making authority, and its members are deemed public officials
subject to the conflict of interest requirements of the Act. On
the other hand, if the Council does not meet any of these tests,
the Council is merely an advisory body and is not subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

Make, Compel or Prevent a Final Governmental Decision

    From the Bylaws and the information you gave us by telephone,
the Council does not appear to possess the authority to make any
final governmental decisions. In fact, this power is reserved
exclusively to the Board of Supervisors. It makes all final
decisions regarding policy at the County level. Because the
Council lacks power to make a final governmental decision,
Subsection (A) is inapplicable.

    Similarly, Subsection (B) does not appear to apply in this
case. Section Four of the Purposes Clause of the Council's Bylaws
specifies that the Council has the power to review and make
recommendations.

Substantive Recommendations Regularly Approved

    Subsection (C) seeks to include within the scope of the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act those commissions and
boards whose recommendations are merely "rubber stamped" by a
public official or governmental agency. If a pattern of "rubber
stamp" approval by any public official or governmental agency
arises, then the commission or board will become a decision-making

Our File No. A-91-503
Page 5


body for the purposes of the Act, and its members will become
subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.²

     In this case, based on the information provided, the Council
makes substantive recommendations to the Board of Supervisors
which have been regularly approved without significant amendment
or modification over an extended period of time.  The
recommendations are substantive.  They include approving or
disapproving local development projects, as well as land use
ordinance changes.  Furthermore, the Council prepares the Land Use
Element of the General Plan Update, and it drafts the policy
statement which guides the planning department in making
amendments to the General Plan.

     In addition to the Council's recommendations being
substantive, you stated that at least two Supervisors have
verbally informed you that they always follow the Council's
recommendations when voting on an issue.  Moreover, the Board of
Supervisors has never voted against a Council recommendation to
the best of your knowledge.

     The Commission has concluded on several occasions that where
an advisory committee makes substantive recommendations to a
governmental agency that are regularly approved without
significant modification, then the members are public officials
under the Act and they are subject to the disclosure and
disqualification provisions.  (See, e.g., Woodbury Advice Letter,
A-90-665; Paley Advice Letter, A-90-583; Korade Advice Letter, A-
89-715; Ball Advice Letter, I-89-671.)

     Here, the Council's recommendations are not regularly
scrutinized and amended prior to approval by the Board of
Supervisors.  In other words, here there is a history indicating
that the Council's recommendations are merely "rubber stamped" by
the Board.

     We conclude, therefore, that the Council is a decision-making
body and that the Council's members are "public officials" as
defined by the Act.  As a result, the members of the Council are
subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the
Act.

     To assist you further, I have enclosed 21 copies of the
Commission's pamphlet entitled "A Guide to the Conflict of
Interest Provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974" and 2
copies of the Political Reform Act (with supplement) pursuant to
your request.


_________________________________________________________________
²    Those requirements would include the adoption of a conflict of
interest code (Sections 87300 - 87313), financial disclosure
requirements, and disqualification in the event of a conflict of
interest.

Our File No. A-91-503
Page 6


I trust this letter addresses your concerns. If you have any
further questions, feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.3

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:CMM:aa

Prepared with the assistance of
Chris Micheli, Legal Intern




3 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

Consequently, the members are subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

Although it was started in the late 1920s, the Council was reactivated in the early 1970s. sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the State of California in 1974. The purpose of the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own economic interest or the economic interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

The General Rule

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests.

Public Official

A public official includes every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a local or state government agency. (Section 82048.) A "public official" includes members of boards and commissions and is defined in Regulation 18700(a)(1) as follows:

(a) Public official at any level of state or local government means every natural person who is a member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency.

(1) Member shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or commissions with decision-making authority. A board or commission possesses decision-making authority whenever:

(A) It may make a final governmental decision;

(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or

(C) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency.

[Emphasis added.]

Accordingly, if the Council meets any of the tests of Regulation 18700(a)(1)(A), (B) or (C), it possesses decision-making authority, and its members are deemed public officials subject to the conflict of interest requirements of the Act. On the other hand, if the Council does not meet any of these tests, the Council is merely an advisory body and is not subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

Make, Compel or Prevent a Final Governmental Decision

From the Bylaws and the information you gave us by telephone, the Council does not appear to possess the authority to make any final governmental decisions. In fact, this power is reserved exclusively to the Board of Supervisors. It makes all final decisions regarding policy at the County level. Because the Council lacks power to make a final governmental decision, Subsection (A) is inapplicable.

Similarly, Subsection (B) does not appear to apply in this case. Section Four of the Purposes Clause of the Council's Bylaws specifies that the Council has the power to review and make recommendations.

Substantive Recommendations Regularly Approved

Subsection (C) seeks to include within the scope of the conflict of interest provisions of the Act those commissions and boards whose recommendations are merely "rubber stamped" by a public official or governmental agency. If a pattern of "rubber stamp" approval by any public official or governmental agency arises, then the commission or board will become a decision-making

body for the purposes of the Act, and its members will become subject to the Act’s conflict of interest provisions.2

In this case, based on the information provided, the Council makes substantive recommendations to the Board of Supervisors which have been regularly approved without significant amendment or modification over an extended period of time. The recommendations are substantive. They include approving or disapproving local development projects, as well as land use ordinance changes. Furthermore, the Council prepares the Land Use Element of the General Plan Update, and it drafts the policy statement which guides the planning department in making amendments to the General Plan.

In addition to the Council’s recommendations being substantive, you stated that at least two Supervisors have verbally informed you that they always follow the Council’s recommendations when voting on an issue. Moreover, the Board of Supervisors has never voted against a Council recommendation to the best of your knowledge.

The Commission has concluded on several occasions that where an advisory committee makes substantive recommendations to a governmental agency that are regularly approved without significant modification, then the members are public officials under the Act and they are subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions. (See, e.g., Woodbury Advice Letter, A-90-665; Paley Advice Letter, A-90-583; Korade Advice Letter, A-89-715; Ball Advice Letter, I-89-671.)

Here, the Council’s recommendations are not regularly scrutinized and amended prior to approval by the Board of Supervisors. In other words, here there is a history indicating that the Council’s recommendations are merely "rubber stamped" by the Board.

We conclude, therefore, that the Council is a decision-making body and that the Council’s members are "public officials" as defined by the Act. As a result, the members of the Council are subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

To assist you further, I have enclosed 21 copies of the Commission’s pamphlet entitled "A Guide to the Conflict of Interest Provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974" and 2 copies of the Political Reform Act (with supplement) pursuant to your request.

2 Those requirements would include the adoption of a conflict of interest code (Sections 87300 - 87313), financial disclosure requirements, and disqualification in the event of a conflict of interest.

I trust this letter addresses your concerns. If you have any further questions, feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.3

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are members of the Cayucos Citizens Advisory Council "public officials" subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION: Because the Cayucos Citizens Advisory Council makes substantive recommendations to the San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors that are regularly approved without significant modification, the Council is a decision-making body and its members are public officials as defined by the Act. Consequently, the members are subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
